result:
Prompt caching lets an LLM API store the internal representation (the computed key/value attention state) of a prefix of your prompt—typically a long, stable system prompt, tool definitions, or documents—so that reused prefixes don't have to be reprocessed on later requests. On a cache hit, the provider skips recomputing those tokens' attention states and reads them from memory instead.

Cached input tokens are discounted because the expensive part—running the tokens through the model's forward pass—was already done and paid for on the first request. Serving from cache is mostly a memory read, so providers charge a fraction of the normal input rate (often ~10%).

One practical thing: put your **stable content first** and your variable content (the user's latest message) last. Caches match on exact prefixes, so keeping unchanging system instructions and context at the front maximizes hit rates and savings.
